data.title: ZAWYA: EFG Hermes concludes advisory on the Accelerated Bookbuild of Valu shares
data.description:
Cairo: EFG Hermes, an EFG Holding company and the leading investment bank in MENA, announced today the successful conclusion of its advisory role on the Accelerated Bookbuild (ABB) secondary share sale in Valu, Egypt’s leading universal financial technology platform. The transaction involves the placement of 53.8 million shares, representing 2.55% of the share capital of Valu.

EFG Hermes acted as the financial advisor and bookrunner on the deal, leveraging its market-leading execution capabilities and integrated sales platform to maintain orderly market performance within an accelerated timeframe. The successful execution of this ABB highlights the Egyptian Exchange’s (EGX) resilience and the continued attractiveness of Egyptian capital markets.

Maged El Ayouti, Co-Head of Investment Banking at EFG Hermes, commented: “This transaction exemplifies our broader efforts to deepen and grow access to liquidity in the region’s capital markets and our increasing activity in follow-on equity transactions that EFG Hermes has executed recently. This follows Valu’s successful listing on the EGX last year and strong share price performance, highlighting the company’s strong momentum and further strengthening investor interest in Valu’s growth story. It stands among a series of offerings that have reignited investor appetite for equities, a continuation of the momentum for new listings and follow-on offerings, following a muted ECM activity during last month against a backdrop of elevated geopolitical risk.”

In 2025, EFG Hermes advised on 18 equity capital market (ECM) transactions, 16 debt capital market (DCM) transactions, and 8 M&A transactions across the region, with notable engagements in Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Oman, and Egypt. These activities highlight the firm’s leadership in regional capital markets and its ongoing role in connecting regional and international investors with strategic opportunities.

About EFG Holding:

EFG Holding (EGX: HRHO.CA – LSE: EFGD) is a financial institution that boasts a legacy of more than 40 years of success in seven countries spanning two continents. Operating within three distinct verticals — the Investment Bank (EFG Hermes), Non-Bank Financial Institutions (NBFI) (EFG Finance), and Commercial Bank (Bank NXT) - the company provides a comprehensive range of groundbreaking financial products and services tailored to meet the needs of a diverse clientele, including individual clients and businesses of all sizes.

EFG Hermes, the leading investment bank in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA), offers extensive financial services, encompassing advisory, asset management, securities brokerage, research, and private equity. In its domestic market, EFG Holding serves as a universal bank, with EFG Finance emerging as the fastest-growing NBFI platform, comprising Tanmeyah, a provider of innovative and integrated financial solutions for micro and small business owners and entrepreneurs, EFG Corp Solutions, which provides leasing and factoring services, Valu, a universal financial technology powerhouse, Bedaya for mortgage finance, Kaf for insurance, and EFG SMEs, which provides financial services for small and medium enterprises. Furthermore, the company delivers commercial banking solutions through Bank NXT, an integrated retail and corporate banking product provider in Egypt.

Proudly present in: Egypt | United Arab Emirates | Saudi Arabia | Kuwait | Bahrain | Kenya | Nigeria

Learn more about us at www.efghldg.com

For further information, please contact:

melgammal@efghldg.com

data.title: ZAWYA: Savills: Egypt real estate remains stable amid regional uncertainty, with limited pricing impact
data.description:
Cairo, Egypt – Egypt’s real estate market continues to show stability despite ongoing regional developments, according to Savills Egypt. While broader geopolitical uncertainty remains, the market is largely operating as usual, with developers maintaining project pipelines and demand levels holding steady.

Savills notes that current pressure points are consistent with previous regional disruptions, with the economic impact on Egypt largely driven by external factors rather than direct exposure. Energy costs are currently the main pressure point, pushing up costs across the market. Despite these pressures, Fitch Ratings stated in a recent report that Egypt’s financial system is entering this phase from a more stable position, supported by strong banking sector fundamentals, including capitalization, profitability, and foreign currency liquidity buffers.

Cost pressures without structural repricing

Catesby Langer-Paget, Head of Savills Egypt, states: “From a real estate perspective, the sector is facing near-term cost pressures, primarily linked to exchange rate movements, energy inputs, and supply chain disruptions. At this stage, we see no indication of a broad repricing of real estate assets. Developers are maintaining pricing discipline while continuing to prioritise sales activity and project delivery. Many had already factored in elevated exchange rates during the volatility of 2024, allowing them to absorb part of the current cost increases.”

Savills also highlights that project pipelines remain active, with continued launches across segments, showing that developers are still moving ahead with plans rather than slowing down.

Demand holds: Real estate continues to act as a safe haven

Demand fundamentals remain intact, according to Savills. Real estate continues to be viewed as a hedge against inflation, with buyers turning to property to protect value.

Buyer activity reflects this trend, with enquiry levels remaining strong. Supported in part by the relative stabilization of the Egyptian pound prior to the conflict, buyers continue allocating capital into real assets.

Egypt’s population growth and ongoing urban expansion continue to support demand across residential and mixed-use projects.

Developer response: Operational continuity and pricing discipline

Savills observes that developers have maintained a steady and measured approach, with no signs of panic or sudden changes in strategy.

The focus remains on sustaining sales, managing costs, and keeping projects on track. Pricing strategies are being approached cautiously, without sharp adjustments.

According to Savills, this reflects a more mature market, where developers are better equipped to manage volatility through improved planning assumptions and disciplined financial models, drawing on experience from previous market cycles.

Sustained regional confidence

As per Savills research, regional investment activity remains stable. GCC developers and investors in Egypt are continuing to advance projects, including large-scale developments along the North Coast.

This points to continued confidence in Egypt’s real estate market and its long-term potential.

Outlook: Duration remains the key variable

Looking ahead, Savills emphasizes that the trajectory will depend on the duration of the current conflict and its impact on currency stability and energy costs.

Overall, the market is expected to remain resilient, with Egypt’s real estate sector supported by underlying demand, more experienced developers, and sustained investor interest.

Savills will continue to monitor developments closely as the situation evolves.

About Savills

Savills plc is a global real estate services provider listed on the London Stock Exchange. Today, the company has a strong global presence, with more than 700 offices and associates and around 42,000 employees worldwide, across the Americas, the UK, Europe, Asia Pacific, Africa, and the Middle East. It provides a wide range of specialized advisory services, in addition to management and transactional services, to clients around the world.

Savills has 50 years of experience in the Middle East and North Africa region, and now operates seven offices across the region, including one in Egypt.

Since its launch in 2019, Savills Egypt has grown rapidly to over 250 professionals across its core divisions, expanding to more than 400 employees including SMR, its dedicated facilities management arm. Savills brings the Egyptian market a wide range of specialist services from property management, project management, strategic consultancy, interior design as well as marketing and corporate services.

Today, Savills Egypt acts as a trusted advisor to many of the country’s leading developers, investors, and corporates, playing a key role in shaping market-defining projects across Egypt.

For more information, please visit www.savills.com.eg.

For media inquiries, please contact:

data.title: Costco at $1,000: Still a Buy or Time to Wait?
data.description:
Costco Wholesale Corporation (Costco) is a global retail giant that operates on a membership-based warehouse club model. Unlike traditional retailers that focus on high markups, this company acts as a collective buyer for its members, leveraging scale to provide deep discounts on a curated selection of goods. By merging its warehouse operations with its retail storefronts (where the warehouse effectively is the store) it eliminates the expense of separate distribution and storage. The business currently maintains a market cap of roughly $440 billion while operating fewer than 1,000 locations worldwide, generating billions in annual free cash flow while holding a massive cash pile that dwarfs its long-term debt.

It is hard to walk past a warehouse these days without thinking about the $1,000 price tag on the stock. We are sitting at undisputed all-time highs, and the momentum has been relentless, with shares up nearly 12% year-to-date. To put that in perspective, while I have been watching nearby competitors like BJ's Wholesale struggle to keep up with a 7.5% gain, and even the retail giant Walmart trailing at 11%, this company just keeps finding new ways to surprise the market. It wasn't that long ago that even legends like Warren Buffett (Trades, Portfolio) walked away from this position; a move many now calculate left about $1.5 billion on the table. But that is the thing about this business: it has consistently proved the doubters wrong, even when it looks expensive. With a five-year gain of over 200%, the track record is undeniable.

COST Data by GuruFocus

However, a great track record doesn't guarantee a great future return, and as an analyst, I have to ask a much sharper question than "is this a good company?" We all know it's a good company. The real question is: If I am allocating capital today for the next five years, is this elite business still the best place for my dollar, or has the runway already been fully paved into the price? When we frame this against other elite retail businesses, we have to decide if we are buying a growth story that still has legs or if we are merely paying a premium (at a forward multiple approaching 50x for a history lesson.

The Membership Model

When I look at the core of this business, I see something that resembles a high-margin subscription service more than a traditional grocery store. The true value is not found in the markups on the shelves, which are kept notoriously low to maintain member trust, but in the recurring membership fees that provide a steady, predictable stream of income. In the most recent quarter, membership income climbed by 13.6%, driven by a combination of fee increases and a growing base of shoppers. Total paid members have reached 82.1 million, and the renewal rate in the United States and Canada is a staggering 92.1%. This level of retention suggests that once a consumer enters the ecosystem, they find it nearly impossible to leave, viewing the annual fee as a minor entry cost for the significant savings they reap throughout the year.

The depth of this loyalty is best illustrated by the behavior of executive members, who now account for over 75% of total sales. These are the most dedicated customers, and their growth; nearly 10% over the prior year; indicates that the company is successfully upselling its existing base into higher-tier memberships. With 147.2 million total cardholders globally, the reach is massive, yet the model remains incredibly concentrated. The company does not try to be everything to everyone; instead, it focuses on a limited number of stock-keeping units, which allows it to command better pricing from suppliers and pass those savings directly to the consumer. This creates a virtuous cycle where low prices drive traffic, traffic drives membership, and membership provides the capital to keep prices lower than the competition.

However, I have to consider whether this growth can be sustained at the same breakneck pace. While the numbers are currently excellent, there is a generational shift occurring that I find worth watching. Younger consumers are beginning to make up a larger portion of the membership base, and their loyalty patterns are different from previous generations. They are more mobile, more digitally focused, and potentially less tied to a single physical location. While the recent 22% jump in e-commerce sales suggests the company is adapting well to the digital age, the long-term challenge will be maintaining that 92% renewal rate as the demographic makeup of the membership base evolves. If younger members view the warehouse model as less essential than their parents did, the high-margin membership engine could eventually start to lose its torque, even if the current results show no signs of that happening yet.

The Efficiency Gap

If we compare this operation to its largest peer, Walmart, the efficiency gap is nothing short of remarkable. I find it telling that while Walmart has almost 10,000 stores, Costco generates roughly $24.4K of net income per employee, which is more than double the $10.4K generated by Walmart. Even more impressive is the revenue per employee, which stands at $822K compared to Walmart's $340K. This is a fundamental difference in how the business is built. By using the warehouse as the store itself, the company significantly reduces the headcount and real estate costs required to operate. They do not need separate teams to move items from a backroom to a shelf because the backroom is the shelf, and the pallets are the displays. This streamlined approach means that they can move massive volumes with a fraction of the traditional retail overhead, allowing them to scale in ways that their peers simply cannot replicate without a total overhaul of their logistics.

COST Data by GuruFocus

This operational leanness allows for a gross margin that sits right around the 13% mark; a figure that would be a death sentence for most other retailers but works perfectly here because of the membership fee cushion. In the recent second quarter, we saw sales grow by 9.1% to over $68 billion, outperforming the broader retail sector. Comparable sales were also strong, rising 7.4% overall, or 6.7% when adjusting for gas price deflation and foreign exchange. When I look at these figures alongside Walmart's reported comparable sales growth of just 4.6%, it becomes clear that this company is taking market share even in a crowded environment. They are winning because they are more efficient, and that efficiency allows them to be the last to raise prices and the first to lower them as commodity inflation cools. The ability to pass through lower prices on staples like eggs and coffee as inflation eases is a direct result of this efficiency, keeping traffic trends positive even when consumer sentiment is shaky.

We also see this efficiency in how they handle logistical disruptions and macro-economic shifts. For instance, management has been proactive in circumventing supply chain issues and the potential impact of tariffs by moving some production lines domestically. Furthermore, a recent federal court ruling regarding certain global tariffs could result in a windfall of refunds. While the exact amount is uncertain, the company has already indicated that any such windfall would be passed back to members through lower prices and improved value. This commitment to the member-first philosophy is what keeps the traffic coming. During the last quarter, worldwide shopping frequency increased by 3.1%, and the average ticket rose by 4.2%, proving that members are not only coming in more often but are also spending more when they do, even as the broader economy shows signs of fatigue. This resilience is a key differentiator when deciding where to allocate long-term capital.

The Strength of the Balance Sheet

One of the most comforting aspects of this investment case is the sheer health of the balance sheet. With $17.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents against only $5.7 billion in long-term debt, this company certainly qualifies. This lopsided ratio gives management an incredible amount of optionality. They are currently planning to deploy between $5.6 billion and $5.8 billion in capital expenditures, much of which is earmarked for the opening of new warehouses. The plan is to open roughly 28 locations in fiscal 2026 and target more than 30 annual openings starting in fiscal 2027. This steady expansion of the physical footprint provides a clear runway for revenue and membership growth for years to come. In a world where many companies are struggling to find high-return projects, the ability to build and immediately fill 30 warehouses a year is a rare growth lever.

This cash pile also fuels a shareholder-friendly policy that is somewhat unique in the retail space. While the stated dividend yield is relatively low at around 0.5%, the company frequently issues large special dividends when cash levels get high. The last one was a $15 per share payment at the end of 2023. Given that the current payout ratio is only about 24%, I expect another significant increase in the regular dividend or perhaps another special dividend announcement within the year. The company also spent roughly $1.15 billion on dividends in the recent quarter alone, while simultaneously buying back 20 million shares over the past year. This balance between returning cash and reinvesting in expansion is why investors view the company as a "fortress" in turbulent times, making it a natural candidate when searching for an "undisputed great business" to hold.

However, I must acknowledge the risk that this massive cash position could eventually lead to less disciplined spending or over-expansion. While the current plan to open 30 stores a year seems measured, the pressure to maintain high growth rates could lead to expansion into less profitable markets or overpriced acquisitions. Moreover, while the debt levels are low, the company still has to navigate a world of rising interest rates and potential labor market softening. If unemployment ticks up significantly, even the most loyal members might start to reconsider their discretionary spending on high-ticket items. We are already seeing some signs of spending fatigue in the U.S., where comparable sales grew only by a couple of percentage points when excluding gas and digital. The company's resilience is being tested, and while it is passing for now, the margin for error is shrinking as the valuation stays elevated.

Longer-term Patterns and Risks

When I zoom out and look at the longer-term trajectory, the stock has gained over 200% in the last five years, significantly outperforming its peers and the broader market. This is the result of a consistent ability to exceed expectations and adapt to changing consumer tastes. A perfect example of this adaptability was the decision to start selling gold bars. While it seemed like a niche move to some, it turned out to be a massive success, with prices for those bars jumping 150% in value since their introduction. This move attracted new customers and drove significant traffic to the website and warehouses, proving that the company knows exactly how to capture the cultural zeitgeist and turn it into revenue. It shows a management team that isn't just "boring" but is strategically nimble enough to find growth in unconventional corners.

Another long-term pattern that deserves attention is the company's commitment to value leadership in staple items. We have seen them lower prices on essential goods like eggs, cheese, coffee, and paper goods as commodity costs have come down. This is part of a long-standing philosophy that builds immense trust with the consumer. In an era where many retailers are accused of keeping prices artificially high, this company is doing the opposite. By being the last to raise and first to lower prices, they ensure that the value destination tag remains firmly attached to their brand. This creates a psychological moat that is incredibly difficult for competitors like BJ's or Target to bridge, regardless of their own pricing strategies. The gold bar phenomenon shows they can innovate, but the question is whether they can do it fast enough to justify a multiple that rivals high-growth tech companies.

However, no business is without its long-term risks. The ongoing geopolitical tension, particularly the conflict involving the U.S. and Iran, poses a threat to global trade and could drive up shipping costs or cause further supply chain disruptions. While the company has shown a knack for navigating these waters, a prolonged or escalating conflict could eventually weigh on margins. Furthermore, the very strength of the brand (its predictability and boring consistency) could be its undoing if it fails to innovate for a new generation of shoppers. We see that they are pushing digitally-enabled sales, which were up 22.6% in the last quarter, but the core business still relies on the physical trip to the warehouse. Any sustained weakening in the labor market could cause unemployment to tick up, potentially slowing the membership growth that serves as the bedrock for the entire valuation.

Valuation Thoughts and Guru Activity

Now we come to the most contentious part of the analysis: the valuation. As an analyst, I find it difficult to ignore that the stock is currently trading at a forward P/E multiple of 47x earnings. For a retail company, that is an eye-watering figure. To put it in perspective, Walmart trades at about 40x forward earnings, Target at 14x, and BJ's at 21x. While I agree that this company deserves to trade at a premium due to its superior business model, higher ROE (currently around 30.3%), and massive cash pile, a 47x multiple suggests that a lot of future perfection is already priced in. We are looking at a Price/Sales ratio of 1.6x, which is almost triple the 0.6x ratio of BJ's Wholesale. This makes the stock richly priced by almost any traditional metric.

COST Data by GuruFocus

The share price has recently hovered around the $1,000 mark, which creates a psychological barrier for many individual investors. In fact, the last time the stock split was 26 years ago, when they did a 2-for-1 split. Given that peers like Walmart and NVIDIA have all seen massive price gains lead to splits recently, I believe a stock split for this company is not just a possibility, but a necessity to improve accessibility. A 10-for-1 or even a 5-for-1 split would bring the price down to a more manageable level and potentially spark a new wave of retail interest. However, a split does not change the underlying valuation. At almost 50x earnings, the stock is still very expensive, and even with expected earnings growth of 10%, it would take years for the fundamentals to catch up to the current price if the multiple contracts.

If I am allocating capital today for a 5-year horizon, I have to compare this to other elite options. Walmart is cheaper and has its own massive momentum, but its ROE and revenue per employee lag significantly. BJ's and Target offer much more "value" in terms of multiples, but they lack the global expansion runway and the absolute safe haven status that this company commands. The real question is whether you want to pay for the runway that this company still has. Unlike more mature dividend aristocrats that rely solely on pricing power and capital efficiency, this company still has a physical expansion lever to pull, with hundreds of more warehouses potentially to be built globally. With only 942 warehouses expected by the end of 2026, there is still a massive world to conquer. But at a 5x PEG ratio, you are paying for every inch of that runway upfront.

It is always useful to see how the guru investors are positioning themselves in a stock like this. Looking at the most recent filings, the picture is somewhat mixed but generally leans toward a hold or slight reduction for the biggest players. Ken Fisher (Trades, Portfolio) remains the largest individual guru holder with over 3.1 million shares, though he only added a marginal 0.99% to his position recently. This suggests that while he still believes in the long-term story, he is not rushing to buy more at these elevated prices. On the other hand, Renaissance Technologies (Trades, Portfolio) made a massive move, increasing its position by over 5,220%, bringing its total to 693,002 shares. This kind of aggressive buying from a quant-heavy firm suggests they see a momentum or technical tailwind that might not be obvious from the fundamentals alone.

However, other institutional funds are heading for the exits or trimming significantly. The Harbor Capital Appreciation Fund (Trades, Portfolio) reduced its holding by 11%, and Jefferies Group (Trades, Portfolio) slashed its position by a massive 86.97%. Ray Dalio (Trades, Portfolio) and Mario Gabelli (Trades, Portfolio) also reduced their stakes by 26% and 22% respectively. This trend of selling into strength by seasoned value and macro investors is a red flag for me. It indicates that those who have made significant gains over the last five years are starting to take chips off the table, likely concerned about the 50x P/E multiple and the potential for a market correction. When the gurus start thinning their ranks, it is usually a sign that the easy money has already been made. They aren't selling out entirely, but they certainly aren't chasing the $1,000 price tag with fresh capital.

There are a few new entrants, like Steven Cohen (Trades, Portfolio) and Lee Ainslie (Trades, Portfolio), who have initiated small new holdings, but these are relatively minor in the grand scheme of the shares outstanding. The fact that the total number of investors in this elite group is small suggests that this is a highly concentrated bet. If a few more of these gurus decide that the valuation has finally disconnected from reality, we could see a wave of institutional selling that puts downward pressure on the stock. For now, the hold sentiment seems to be the consensus among the big players, with most content to sit on their existing piles and wait for a better entry point or a clearer signal of the next growth phase. If the smartest capital in the room is standing pat or trimming, we should be very cautious about being the ones to jump in at the top.

Final Thoughts

After weighing all the evidence, I find myself in a bit of a quandary. On one hand, this is arguably one of the best-run businesses in the world. Its membership model is a work of genius, its balance sheet is a fortress, and its operational efficiency makes its competitors look sluggish by comparison. The company has shown it can thrive in inflationary environments and navigate supply chain crises. If I were looking for a stock to hold for the next twenty years and never look at the price, this would be at the top of my list. The fundamentals are rock solid, and the long-term runway for expansion is clear.

On the other hand, as an analyst looking at the here and now, I cannot ignore that the stock is currently trading at a 50x forward P/E multiple. When I look at the capital allocation choice, I see a business that is too good to sell but too expensive to buy. Compared to Walmart or BJ's, you are getting a significantly better operation, but you are paying a massive premium for it that likely caps your 5-year upside to the mid-to-high single digits. The valuation has reached a point where it is priced for perfection, leaving almost no room for the inevitable macro-economic hiccups or competitive pressures that will eventually arise. We are seeing some signs of cooling in U.S. consumer spending, and while the company is currently outperforming its peers, it is not immune to a broader economic downturn. The risk of a significant price pullback in a market correction is high.

My final recommendation is a Hold. For those who already own the stock, there is no reason to sell a winner of this caliber, especially with the potential for a special dividend or a stock split on the horizon. However, for prospective investors looking to initiate a new position, the current price is simply too high for comfort. If you are allocating new capital today, you are likely to find better risk-adjusted returns elsewhere in the retail sector or in other "elite" businesses that aren't trading at all-time highs with a 5.0 PEG ratio. I believe the prudent move is to wait for a more attractive entry point; perhaps a dip back toward the $850 or $900 level; before putting new capital to work. This remains a five-star business currently trading at a six-star price.

data.title: ONEOK: Midstream's Best Risk-Adjusted Value Play
data.description:
Thesis

ONEOK has doubled its EBITDA in just two years and the market hasn't caught up. At approximately $87 per share, OKE trades at a roughly 20% discount to its five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple, offering investors a rare combination: a nearly 5% dividend yield growing 34% annually, a fee-based business model generating $8 billion in adjusted EBITDA, and a multi-billion-dollar growth backlog set to unlock significant free cash flow starting in 2027.

The stock's selloff driven by conservative 2026 guidance reflecting $5560 WTI assumptions has created a compelling entry point for a fundamentally transformed company. ONEOK today operates the longest refined products pipeline system in America, processes over 5.5 Bcf/d of natural gas, and has captured $475 million in acquisition synergies that already exceed original targets by more than 2x. At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, the risk-reward skews decisively bullish.

The Defining KPI: Adjusted EBITDA Growth

The single most compelling metric for the OKE bull case is adjusted EBITDA growth. ONEOK has delivered 12 consecutive years of EBITDA growth from 2013 through 2025, compounding at a greater than 16% annualized rate. From the COVID trough of $2.7 billion in 2020, EBITDA has tripled to $8.0 billion a five-year CAGR of approximately 24%. No other large-cap midstream company has matched this trajectory.

The 2026 guidance of $8.1 billion at midpoint essentially flat year-over-year represents what management has framed as a consolidation year built on conservative $5560 WTI assumptions. If WTI averages closer to $65 than the guided $57.50 midpoint, EBITDA could exceed $8.5 billion and resume the growth trajectory. The EBITDA bridge shows roughly $335 million in positive contributions from volume growth, synergies, and project completions, offset by $375 million in headwinds from lower commodity price assumptions.

Financial Summary

Synergy Capture: Ahead of Schedule and Accelerating

The strongest near-term bullish signal is ONEOK's synergy performance. When the $18.8 billion Magellan acquisition was announced, management projected at least $200 million in annual base synergies. The results have blown past those targets: through year-end 2025, ONEOK achieved $475 million in cumulative EBITDA synergies more than double the original base target. Including non-cash compensation and supply chain savings, total life-to-date identified synergies reached $707 million.

Management has guided for an additional $150 million in incremental synergies in 2026, bringing the cumulative EBITDA total to approximately $625 million by year-end 2026, with a stated goal of exceeding $700 million. The synergy sources are diversified: commercial and operations synergies contributed $321 million, asset optimization added $175 million, batching and blending efficiencies generated $143 million, and corporate cost reductions delivered $154 million. Market share gains, still in early innings at just $3 million to date, are expected to add $34 million in 2026 alone suggesting the commercial synergy runway has significant upside remaining.

An additional and often overlooked benefit: the Magellan deal created a tax basis step-up worth an estimated $3.0 billion (NPV of approximately $1.5 billion), which defers meaningful cash tax payments until 2029. This effectively increases free cash flow available for debt reduction and shareholder returns throughout the integration period.

Growth Drivers ? P&L Bridge

ONEOK's current capex cycle represents its most ambitious organic growth buildout in company history, with total capital expenditures of $3.15 billion in 2025 and guidance of $2.7$3.2 billion for 2026. Each growth vector feeds directly into the income statement:

Management projects historical EBITDA multiples of 46x on organic growth capital. At the guided approximately $2.4 billion in 2026 growth capex, this implies $400600 million in incremental EBITDA as projects reach full contribution the bulk of which arrives in 20272028. This is the inflection the market is underpricing.

Peer Comparison: Discounted Despite Superior Growth Track Record

At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, OKE trades at a meaningful discount to Williams Companies (12.4x), Kinder Morgan (14.5x), and Targa Resources (12.5x) despite having delivered the highest EBITDA growth in the peer group over the past three years. Only Enterprise Products Partners (9.5x) and Energy Transfer (5.8x, reflecting its MLP structure) trade cheaper. The discount reflects market concerns about leverage and the pause in EBITDA growth, but both are temporary conditions that should resolve as capex normalizes and projects contribute.

Valuation Sanity Check

At approximately $86.93, OKE trades at 10.8x NTM EV/EBITDA and 15.5x NTM earnings. Applying a 12x multiple a modest premium reflecting successful integration, still below the historical 1314x average to the top end of 2026 EBITDA guidance ($8.3 billion) yields an enterprise value of $99.6 billion. After subtracting approximately $33 billion in net debt and dividing by 630 million shares, this implies a fair value of approximately $106 per share roughly 22% upside from current levels. Add the 4.9% dividend yield, and total return potential exceeds 25%.

The annualized dividend of $4.28 carries 56 consecutive years of payments. Management's stated 34% annual growth target, combined with a $2 billion share repurchase authorization, provides a clear capital return framework. The forward P/E of 15.5x against expected EPS of $5.45 further underscores the value relative to peers like Williams (24x) and Kinder Morgan (24.4x).

Risks

The bear case centers on three interrelated concerns. First, leverage at 3.8x Debt/EBITDA is the highest among major C-corp midstream peers and leaves limited margin for EBITDA erosion the interest coverage ratio of 3.3x is adequate but not robust. However, ONEOK retired $3.1 billion in debt in 2025 alone, and the absence of cash taxes until 2029 supports a clear deleveraging glide path.

Second, the flat 2026 EBITDA guidance has raised questions about whether the growth streak is ending rather than pausing. This reflects management's conservative $5560 WTI assumptions at prices just $510 above the guided range, EBITDA would exceed $8.3 billion and resume the growth trajectory.

Third, Permian Basin drilling headwinds could pressure volumes if crude prices remain in the mid-$50s, with rig counts already down roughly 33% from late 2022 peaks. However, structural production growth from rising gas-to-oil ratios means NGL and gas volumes can grow even with fewer rigs operating. The integration complexity of absorbing three major acquisitions simultaneously ($25B+ in deal value) is arguably the most material risk, yet the synergy performance to date validates management's execution capability.

Conclusion

ONEOK represents a rare combination of value, yield, and growth in today's midstream sector. The company has executed one of the most ambitious transformation strategies in energy infrastructure tripling EBITDA in five years while shifting to a 90% fee-based model and is now being penalized for a single year of conservative guidance built on bearish commodity assumptions.At 10.8x forward EV/EBITDA, a 4.9% yield, and a clear path to accelerating free cash flow generation in 2027, the risk-reward skews decisively bullish. The $475 million in synergies already captured validates the acquisition strategy, the $5 billion growth backlog provides multi-year earnings visibility, and the approaching capex inflection should catalyze both deleveraging and enhanced shareholder returns. For investors willing to look past a transitory guidance disappointment, OKE at $87 offers roughly 22% upside to fair value plus a growing nearly 5% dividend making it the most compelling risk-adjusted opportunity in large-cap midstream today.

data.description:

The move follows a recent upgrade in the company’s long-term credit ratings to the highest “AAA (Stable)” category by agencies including CRISIL Ratings, ICRA, India Ratings & Research and CARE Ratings.

According to the company, the upgraded ratings apply to its bank loan facilities, non-convertible debentures, subordinated debt and fixed deposit programmes, reflecting improved credit strength and stability.

FD rates revised across tenures

Shriram Finance said it has adjusted interest rates across select tenures for deposits up to ₹10 crore. For instance, the rate for a 12-month deposit has been reduced from 7.00% to 6.75% per annum. A 15-month digital-only deposit continues to offer a higher rate compared to shorter tenures.

data.title: Bitcoin Is the Ultimate AI-Era Scarcity Asset, Says Macro Veteran Wall Street Strategist Eyeing 3% To 5% Portfolio Allocations
data.description:
Key points:
  • Jordi Visser said on Saturday that Bitcoin was emerging as a “critical scarcity” asset as artificial intelligence drives shortages in compute and infrastructure.
  • He argued markets are at an inflection point, with capital moving away from traditional software and toward scarcity-linked assets like Bitcoin and miners.
  • Visser pointed to a “persistent bid” from institutions, citing flows into Strategy, Morgan Stanley’s exchange-traded fund traction, and growing access via major platforms.

Bitcoin (BTC) is emerging as a “critical scarcity” asset in the age of artificial intelligence (AI), with potential to reach 3% to 5% allocations in client portfolios, macro investor Jordi Visser said on Saturday.

Speaking on the Pomp Podcast hosted by entrepreneur Anthony Pompliano, Visser, who has over three decades of market experience and currently serves as the Head of Research in his firm, 22v, said that the rapid growth of AI was driving shortages in key resources such as compute and infrastructure, reshaping how investors think about value.

He said markets are approaching an “inflection point,” where capital is beginning to shift away from traditional software businesses and toward assets linked to scarcity, including Bitcoin and crypto miners.

Visser also pointed to what he described as a “persistent bid” for Bitcoin, driven by growing institutional participation. He cited strong inflows into Strategy (MSTR), which acquired more than $1 billion worth of Bitcoin in the first two weeks of April, traction in exchange-traded funds (ETFs) offered through firms like Morgan Stanley (MS), and expanding access through major brokerage platforms. The trend is also being reinforced by broader moves on Wall Street, with Goldman Sachs recently expanding its Bitcoin ETF offerings.

Visser argued that financial advisors and private wealth managers were likely to formalize Bitcoin’s role in portfolios, with allocations in the 3%-5% range becoming more common as adoption increases.

Bitcoin’s price was trading at $75,072, down by 2% over the past 24 hours. On Stocktwits, the retail sentiment around BTC remained in the ‘bullish’ zone, while chatter around it stayed in the ‘high’ levels over the past day.

AI-Driven Scarcity And Bitcoin’s Role

Visser’s comments build on arguments he outlined in a recent essay, in which he explained that artificial intelligence was not creating pure abundance but rather driving new forms of scarcity in energy and infrastructure.

He argued that while AI may reduce costs in software and labor, it is simultaneously increasing demand for physical resources, creating constraints that traditional monetary policy cannot easily address. In that environment, Visser said Bitcoin stands out as a scarce digital asset, positioned to benefit from both macroeconomic shifts and rising demand for alternatives to traditional financial systems.

data.title: Aluminum giant Alcoa to sell dormant smelter to Bitcoin miner NYDIG: Report
data.description:
US aluminium giant Alcoa is reportedly nearing a deal to offload its long-idle Massena East smelter in upstate New York to Bitcoin mining firm New York Digital Investment Group (NYDIG).

The company is in advanced discussions and expects the transaction to close “in the middle part of this year,” CEO Bill Oplinger told Bloomberg on Friday. The site, located along the St. Lawrence River, has been inactive since 2014 after Alcoa shut it down amid rising energy costs and global competition.

Built for 24/7 heavy industrial operations, aluminum smelters come with pre-existing substations, transmission lines and high-capacity grid connections. That makes them attractive targets for Bitcoin miners and data center operators, who often spend years securing similar infrastructure approvals from scratch.

Massena East also benefits from hydropower supplied by the New York Power Authority, a key draw for energy-intensive computing firms seeking low-cost and lower-carbon power sources.

US smelters reborn as crypto, AI data centers

The potential sale comes amid a broader trend across the US, where retired industrial sites are being repurposed for digital infrastructure. Earlier this year, Century Aluminum sold its Hawesville smelter in Kentucky to TeraWulf for $200 million, with plans to convert it into a high-performance computing and AI facility rather than traditional industrial use.

Meanwhile, NYDIG has been growing its footprint in Bitcoin (BTC) mining infrastructure. The firm, owned by Stone Ridge, already holds a stake in Coinmint, which operates mining hardware at the same campus under a long-term lease.

Last year, Crusoe Energy also agreed to sell its Bitcoin mining business, including its digital flare mitigation operations, to NYDIG.

Bitcoin miners pivot to AI

NYDIG’s renewed push into Bitcoin mining comes as other miners are increasingly pivoting toward AI and cloud computing as shrinking margins in mining push them to diversify revenue streams.

Earleir this year, MARA Holdings acquired a 64% stake in French infrastructure company Exaion, giving the company a foothold in AI services. Other miners, including Hive, Hut 8, TeraWulf and Iren, are also repurposing mining facilities into data centers, while some, such as CoreWeave, have fully transitioned into AI-focused infrastructure.

LDO Info

The Lido project is a liquid staking platform for PoS (Proof-of-Stake) cryptocurrencies, which enables users to stake their tokens on Lido and receive tokenized versions of their staked assets at a 1:1 ratio. Lido supports Ethereum 2.0 staking and other Layer 1 PoS blockchains, such as Solana, Polygon, Polkadot, and Kusama. By issuing these tokenized assets, users maintain liquidity and are able to participate in DeFi activities for additional yield while earning staking rewards.

The mechanism of operation for Lido revolves around its unique liquid staking solution. Users can stake any amount of PoS assets, addressing the issues of illiquidity, complexity, and centralization present in traditional PoS staking. When a user deposits assets to Lido, they’re staked on the relevant blockchain via Lido’s smart contract, which manages deposits, withdrawals, fund delegation, fees, token minting and burning, and more. Users receive a tokenized version of their deposited funds (stAsset tokens), which can be used in DeFi protocols and dApps to earn additional rewards.

Regarding the LDO token, it’s important to note that LDO is the native governance token of the Lido protocol. Holders of LDO tokens have the power to vote on proposals in the Lido DAO (Decentralized Autonomous Organization). The DAO governs the protocol and makes crucial decisions regarding its operation, ensuring alignment with stakeholder interests. The DAO manages fee parameters and distribution, addition and removal of node operators, and other aspects of the protocol, maintaining transparency and decentralization.

data.title: Fire in Malaysia's Sabah destroys 1,000 homes, thousands displaced
data.description:
Thousands of people have been displaced after a fire destroyed around 1,000 homes in a coastal village in Malaysia's Sabah state on Borneo island on Sunday, the fire department said.

Authorities were notified of the fire in Sandakan district at around 1.32 a.m. (1732 GMT), the district's fire and rescue chief Jimmy Lagung said in a statement.

“Strong winds and the close proximity of the houses caused the fire to spread rapidly, while low tide conditions also made it difficult to obtain an open water source,” Lagung said.

The fire broke out in one of Sabah's water villages, which feature wooden houses built on stilts and are home to some of the country's poorest communities, including many stateless and indigenous groups.

Over 9,000 residents have been affected by the fire though no deaths have been reported, Sandakan police said, according to state news agency Bernama.

Malaysia's Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said the federal government was coordinating with Sabah authorities to provide basic assistance and temporary relocation for those affected.

"The priority now is the safety of the victims and immediate assistance on the ground," he said in a Facebook post.

data.title: Charles Schwab, Citadel Circle Prediction Markets While Bernstein Calls $1 Trillion Boom — Even As Regulators Close In: Report
data.description:
Key points:
  • Charles Schwab and Citadel Securities are reportedly planning to enter prediction markets. 
  • Schwab CEO Rick Wurster said the company could easily launch an offering even though there isn't much client demand right now.
  • As the sector grows and comes under scrutiny, Schwab and Citadel look at prediction markets.

Charles Schwab (SCHW) and Citadel Securities are reportedly exploring a potential entry into prediction markets, a $1 trillion market.

Rick Wurster, the CEO of Schwab, said on an investor call that the company is likely to add a prediction market offering at some point, according to a report. He said it would be easy to add to its current platform. Wurster said that the company plans to closely look at the opportunity, even though there doesn't seem to be much client demand right now.

Wurster said that Schwab would stay away from areas like sports, politics, and entertainment, stressing that any offering would have to fit with the company's long-term investment goals. He also said that retail bettors have had bad results in the past, which is another reason to be careful.

Charles Schwab's price closed 0.3% down on Friday. On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around SCHW remained in an ‘extremely bullish’ zone, while chatter remained at high levels.

Citadel Securities Sees Potential In Event-Based Contracts

According to President Jim Esposito, Citadel Securities is also keeping an eye on changes in the space. He said liquidity remains low but could improve over time, according to the report. Esposito said that Citadel is also not looking into sports-related markets, but it does see potential in event-based contracts linked to major political or economic events. He said that these kinds of instruments could be used as hedging tools by both institutional and retail investors, especially when big events could affect how well their portfolios do. But neither company has set a date for the launch.

In March, platforms like Kalshi and Polymarket had tens of billions of dollars in trading volume each month. Last month, Ark Invest partnered with Kalshi to provide analytical insight to traders. Cathie Wood, CEO and CIO of Ark Invest, called it "the beginnings of bringing active equity management back into style." Kalshi's event contracts and prediction markets focus on events that have a direct effect on the equity market, said Wood. She believes that as more people watch and trade around these events, they may become interested in investing in related stocks. This could lead to some money moving out of passive index portfolios and into more active strategies.

Bernstein (AB) predicted that the volume of event contracts could reach about $240 billion by the end of 2026 and grow into a $1 trillion market by 2030. But in the US, the industry is facing increasing regulatory pressure. Some state officials have taken platforms to court over claims of illegal sports betting, and some members of Congress have expressed worry about the possibility of insider trading and the integrity of the market.

data.title: The Must-Have Item in Silicon Valley Is a $178 Sweater With a CEO's Face — WSJ
data.description:
By Robbie Whelan

SAN JOSE — Hardik Nahata was browsing merchandise at Nvidia's annual conference when an item caught his eye: a green sweater with a cartoon depiction of Jensen Huang, the chip giant's chief executive, emblazoned on the front.

He tried on the sweater but lost his nerve at the price tag of $178 — roughly the same price as a share of Nvidia stock at the time. But the 28-year-old software engineer from San Francisco was tempted.

"Jensen Huang is like the Taylor Swift of Silicon Valley," said Nahata, who designs AI agents for PayPal. "He has that aura about him."

In most industries, wearing gear splashed with the face of a CEO would invite ridicule. In Silicon Valley, there's a booming market for merch that says, "I worship a tech nerd."

Palantir sells a $75 T-shirt bearing the face of the company's CEO, Alex Karp. Another defense-tech company, Anduril, sells flower-print Hawaiian shirts inspired by founder Palmer Luckey's attire. The first drops of the shirt, which costs $79.99, sold out.

Several e-commerce sites offer leather motorcycle jackets advertised as similar to those worn by Huang and Tesla billionaire Elon Musk, with prices starting at around $140.

"Be the ultimate fashion king with our leather jacket that lets you be the debonair personality in the room," reads the listing on Leather Cult for the $250 "Jensen Huang Leather Jacket."

The outfits actually worn by Huang have become increasingly luxurious — the Tom Ford motorcycle jacket he sported while giving a keynote speech in 2024, made of black calf leather and embossed to look like lizard skin, retails for $8,990.

Tech CEOs used to be literal nerds, owning if not embracing their unstylishness. (Think Bill Gates and early- Jeff Bezos.) Now many are pushing a more brash, rebellious, bro vibe, and their admirers and customers are buying in.

Startup founders often share a "brazen, somewhat bizarre personality" that fans want to connect with, said Alisa Cohn, an executive coach who has worked with tech CEOs. Putting their faces on corporate merchandise is a sign that the companies and their PR teams want people to associate the startups with the personas of their CEOs, she said.

"It's silly and funny. It's like, 'Let your freak flag fly,'" Cohn said. "But it's also about building a cult."

Probably no company hypes the tech-boss-as-hero ethos as unapologetically as Palantir. Some posters in the company's Reddit community found that out when they dared suggesting that donning a Karp tee was anything but cool.

"Unpopular opinion: all these merch posts are so 'fan boy' and extra cringe," one user wrote.

Karp's fans rallied.

"When is the robe and hood coming out?" one loyalist wrote. "People say we're a cult so we should just embrace it."

"For Me, its easy to ignore what people think," another responded.

He later wrote, "Not trying to please anyone. Just spending money where i want," and added, "PTFB" — a meme acronym from the company's Discord channel which stands for "Palantir's the f — ing best."

For all their fame, most tech CEOs are nerdy engineers at heart — and that's part of the point. Putting their names and faces on clothing is a kitschy, ironic way of showing they are in on the joke, said Niki Norton, founder of Southern Merch and Marketing, a Louisiana consulting firm.

But underneath the self-effacement often lies a healthy ego. The AI boom has made Nvidia the world's most valuable company and Huang a " kingmaker" often seen with President Trump and other heads of state. His conference keynote ran for more than two hours.

Karine Hsu attended the final day of the conference and bought one of the sweaters featuring "TJ," for Toy Jensen, a cute digital avatar of the CEO. "I wouldn't say this sweater is my personal fashion choice — like, I wouldn't rock it everywhere I go — but I was excited to buy it, " said Hsu, who runs a tech branding firm.

An Nvidia spokesperson declined to comment.

On the line of limited-edition T-shirts Palantir launched last year, CEO Karp appears in watercolor, neck muscles taut and eyes hidden by dark sunglasses, above his favorite motto, "Dominate."

The shirts, which cost $75 each, sold out in hours, said Eliano Younes, who runs Palantir's corporate merchandise business.

"In many ways, he is a pioneer in the same light as R&B artists and hip-hop artists back in the day and athletes like Michael Jordan and Kobe Bryant," Younes said of Karp.

Mark Zuckerberg took the cult of the founder to a new level when he appeared onstage in 2024 at a Meta Platforms conference wearing a boxy black T-shirt that read, "Aut Zuck Aut Nihil." a play on the Latin phrase "Aut Caesar Aut Nihil," which roughly translates to "All Caesar or Nothing."

Zuckerberg, who has increasingly flirted with high fashion in recent years, created the shirt with L.A. designer Mike Amiri. Neither Meta nor Amiri sells the shirt, but a version is on Amazon for $15.99.

It has zero reviews.

Write to Robbie Whelan at robbie.whelan@wsj.com

data.title: The Hidden Factor in Choosing a College: Did It Rain on Your Tour? — WSJ
data.description:
By Roshan Fernandez

Researchers have spent years pondering what drives college decisions, including older siblings' choices, campus amenities or even whether the football team just won big.

Now, a new study reveals another influence that matters more than you would expect: The weather on your college tour.

Don't underestimate drizzle. Researchers at Amherst College found that poor weather on a campus visit actually reduces a student's likelihood of applying — and the impact is notable, according to a study published by the National Bureau of Economic Research.

Applications dipped 10.1% when a tour was hot and 5.9% when it was cold, relative to moderate temperatures. They also dipped 8.3% when there was precipitation and 4.9% when it was cloudy, compared with sunny days.

"This isn't just a story people tell," said Olivia Feldman, an Amherst senior and co-author of the paper. "It's actually something that exists" and affects "one of the most important decisions many people make in their lives."

Feldman gets it. She recalls that her own college decision involved some factors that "weren't entirely rational." She toured some schools that happened to be cold or rainy, which kept her from fully picturing herself having a good experience there. It's a dynamic she and her co-authors call "the importance of 'feel' " in picking a college.

The Amherst researchers — Feldman, economics professor Joshua Hyman and Matthew McGann, Amherst's dean of admission and financial aid — drew on about eight years of data from an unnamed "highly-selective institution" in the Northeast, excluding the Covid-19 period when in-person tours stopped. They matched tour-attendance records to application logs, and restricted their sample to participants who registered for a tour at least 10 days in advance because those students were unlikely to have chosen a date based on the forecast.

They controlled for variables like time of day and month, and defined "hot" or "cold" conditions using deviations from averages.

The study found that where you grow up also matters. During a cold spell, students from warmer states are 14.6% less likely to apply.

McGann suggests these findings can help campus tour guides still "give a really A-plus visitor experience" when the weather is crummy.

"Maybe it cracks 90 degrees and we bring out the ice cream sandwiches," McGann offered, as a "hedge against potential adverse impacts." They are also considering mentioning the study during tours to make prospective applicants aware of their own unconscious biases.

Other schools already deploy creative strategies against the elements. In warm North Carolina, High Point University often ferries visitors in golf carts. The University of Minnesota Twin Cities might show off "Gopher Way," its elaborate network of tunnels and skyways.

Other simple fixes: distributing university-branded umbrellas in the rain, or sending pre-tour weather alerts so families can adjust expectations or reschedule.

Still, the Amherst researchers found that while bad weather hurts applications, it had no notable impact on whether students who did apply actually enrolled.

Write to Roshan Fernandez at roshan.fernandez@wsj.com

data.title: Private Credit Is on the Hunt for Credit-Card Debt — WSJ
data.description:
By AnnaMaria Andriotis

When Wells Fargo told the fintech Bilt that it would no longer be the lender for its rent-rewards credit card, Bilt scrambled to find another large bank partner. When that failed, Bilt wound up with private-credit funding.

In February, Bilt struck a deal to move roughly $1.2 billion of credit-card balances with funding arranged by a group including Blue Owl Capital and Stone Point Capital as well as Goldman Sachs and TD, according to people familiar with the deal.

The companies also agreed to fund hundreds of millions of dollars of credit-card balances that Bilt cardholders will incur in the future, the people said.

Consumer debt has become one of the hottest categories in private credit, increasingly sought after by funds and investment arms of insurance companies on the hunt for high-yielding investments.

Private credit is in focus on Wall Street right now because of the loans that fund managers have made to software and other companies, often as part of private-equity buyouts, that are now running into trouble. Investors are pulling money away.

In consumer debt, the private-credit engine is powering a variety of companies including financial-technology firms to turn out more and more loans.

Imagine a Harley-Davidson customer at the dealer who takes out a loan to purchase a new motorcycle. The loan is provided by Harley-Davidson Financial Services, which has a deal to sell about two-thirds of its new loans to KKR and the bond giant Pimco. Funds run by those firms get a debt security backed by the hog, and Harley gets to make another loan to the next customer quickly. The customer sees no apparent difference, as Harley continues servicing the loan.

The same thing is happening when consumers purchase a mattress with financing from buy-now-pay-later firm Affirm, sign up for a student loan from Sallie Mae or borrow for their ATV from Octane Lending.

Private-credit funds held $350 billion of consumer-loan balances last year, compared with less than $200 billion in 2019, according to estimates from Jefferies. Those sums are a mixture of previously originated loans that lenders unload to private credit and increasingly so-called forward-flow arrangements, which involve agreements to buy future loans that haven't been originated, similar to the Bilt and Harley pacts.

Much of this debt is personal loans that are often unsecured and that consumers can use for almost anything they want to buy.

Last year, about a quarter of newly originated personal loans were funded by private-credit forward-flow arrangements, up from about 6% the year before, according to Jefferies. The funding mechanism is also picking up for auto and private student loans.

"Private credit is looking to broaden its addressable market, which has historically been skewed towards the commercial side," said Mike Taiano, vice president in the financial institutions group at Moody's Ratings. "It's a diversification play to some degree."

Making this funding possible has been the steady stream of capital coming from investors into private-credit funds and insurers that need to be invested quickly.

Fintechs and other nonbanks are attracted to these deals because they lack large balance sheets and need to grow by originating more loans. They can remain "capital light" and less levered since they are unloading large chunks of debt, often at a premium.

Among the deals: TPG this year agreed that it would buy around $2.4 billion of mostly personal loans from OneMain Financial, which lends to people who typically don't have high credit scores. Late last year, KKR entered into a partnership with private student loan lender Sallie Mae that involves buying at least $2 billion of new loans each year for three years.

Blue Owl now has a dedicated asset-backed finance strategy after buying alternative asset manager Atalaya in late 2024. It entered into nearly $18 billion of forward-flow agreements last year across several lenders including PayPal, SoFi and LendingClub, according to data tracked by Jefferies.

Insurance companies, many of which are now owned by or affiliated with private investment funds, are also expanding. Affirm last year signed forward-flow agreements for roughly up to $9.75 billion from Liberty Mutual Investments, the investment firm of Liberty Mutual; PGIM, the asset-management business of Prudential Financial; and New York Life. The funding is being used for Affirm's monthly installment loans.

Some private-credit executives say consumer debt can be less risky, pointing to the current relatively low delinquency rates on credit cards and other consumer loans. They also find comfort knowing the credit-score and other underwriting requirements around the loans that they buy from consumer lenders.

Others are bearish on consumers, especially those who don't have mortgages. They point to inflation, wage stagnation and a slowing job market, all of which can reduce consumers' ability to pay off debts.

The chase for consumer loans has also contributed to the recent headline concerns. A mortgage lender in the U.K., Market Financial Solutions, grew rapidly in recent years pledging its loans to private-credit firms. But it imploded this year, and firms have accused it of using loans as collateral several times.

Stone Ridge Asset Management, which has an interval fund with consumer and small-business loans from fintech lenders, has faced particularly high redemption requests.

A fintech addiction

Nonbank lenders have traditionally relied on warehouse financing, which functions like a line of credit, to make loans to consumers. Forward flow is an extra mechanism to fund loans. In an ideal scenario, the mix of funding sources gives the lender the ability to originate more or larger loans to consumers.

Octane Lending, which finances motorcycles, ATVs, RVs and other outdoor recreational equipment, entered into up to $700 million of agreements with New York Life, Equitable and a unit of MetLife. Octane expects north of $2 billion in new agreements from insurers to come together by the end of the second quarter.

The goal, said Chief Executive Officer Jason Guss, is to continue growing at a reasonable pace without getting overlevered itself. He also wants his firm to have capital on the sidelines in case of an economic slowdown.

"Quite a few fintechs are addicted to forward flow," said Jordan Miller, CEO of fintech Yendo, which issues credit cards backed by collateral including people's cars and homes and receives much of its funding from private credit.

It isn't all upside, fintech and private-credit executives say. Providers of forward-flow arrangements can pull those deals easily — citing reasons ranging from increased delinquencies to macroeconomic conditions.

In the Bilt deal, the credit-card debt was moved to Fidem Financial, which acts as a balance sheet and gets funding for the program from Goldman, TD, Blue Owl and Stone Point. (Stone Point is also an owner of Fidem.)

Shortly before the Bilt deal was scheduled to be finalized in early February, Blue Owl raised concerns: It wouldn't continue with the deal, it told parties involved in it, unless several things occurred.

Blue Owl wanted a guarantee that would involve its receiving the first tens of millions of dollars of profit generated by the credit-card program and wanted to dig more into the feasibility of Bilt's forward-looking projections, according to people familiar with the matter.

Blue Owl blessed the deal on the eve before the card program was scheduled to transfer its balances away from Wells Fargo.

Write to AnnaMaria Andriotis at annamaria.andriotis@wsj.com

data.title: Intel Is Making Progress. But It Isn't Out of the Woods Yet — Heard on the Street — WSJ
data.description:
By Dan Gallagher

Intel still has a long way to go to reclaim its former glory. The problem is that investors are acting like that's already happened.

The storied chip maker's stock price has soared 88% so far this year, and more than tripled over the last 12 months. That has taken the company's market capitalization close to the $350 billion mark for the first time since 2000, when it was the undisputed leader of both designing and manufacturing the most advanced semiconductors on the planet. Intel also now trades for more than 130 times its projected earnings for this year, far above the peak multiple of 60 times that the stock briefly touched during the dot-com bubble.

That is a risky price for a company still trying to pull off an ambitious turnaround. And even if fully successful, Intel's stock is pricing in a level of earnings power that looks hard to achieve, given the company's shifting business model and the competitive realities of the AI chip market.

Intel is scrambling to catch up to TSMC in chip manufacturing technology after ceding its lead several years ago. At the same time, the company is trying to pull off a complex business model shift, whereby it designs its own chips while also manufacturing chips designed by others. And it is trying to win back share for the processors it designs for servers and personal computers.

All these are still works in progress, though some notable progress has been made. Intel struck a deal in September with Nvidia to co-develop a new class of chips for data centers and laptops. That deal kicked off the stock's recent run, but developments since then have given the shares even more juice. Even social-media posts by President Trump have made an impact; Intel's stock jumped 11% in one day in early January after Trump praised Intel Chief Executive Lip-Bu Tan and said the U.S. government "is proud to be a shareholder of Intel" on his Truth Social network.

In the past month, Intel landed a major chip supply deal with Google and was named a partner in Elon Musk's Terrafab project, which aims to build a massive new chip production facility in Texas that would serve the needs of both Tesla and SpaceX.

But the Terrafab announcement was light on details, beyond saying Intel would help with "refactor silicon fab technology" for the project. "Frankly we aren't even sure what that means," wrote Stacy Rasgon, an 18-year chip analyst at Bernstein who has a Ph.D. in chemical engineering.

One thing that really is going Intel's way is a shift in AI computing. That market has so far been dominated by graphics processing chips, or GPUs, that are mostly supplied by Nvidia. But while GPUs are still superior for training AI models, newer AI tools such as agents require a process known as inferencing that is better suited for central processing chips, or CPUs. Those types of chips have typically served as the main "brain" of servers and personal computers that have historically been Intel's strongest markets.

"This dynamic should provide a renewed growth engine for CPUs that have until recently been viewed by many as ex-growth at best, and at worst left for dead by investors," TD Cowen analyst Josh Buchalter wrote in a report earlier this month. New Street Research projects that shipments of CPUs for AI servers will average 43% annual growth through 2030.

Not all of this will flow to Intel. Rival AMD has gained a lot of market share in server CPUs over the past few years at Intel's expense. Still, Intel's strong position in the server market should help the company capture some of the new momentum, especially as demand also grows for replacements of traditional non-AI servers. Analysts expect Intel's data center segment to increase revenue by 13% this year compared with an anemic 2% growth projected for the company's PC business, according to estimates from FactSet.

But even improving server sales are unlikely to return Intel to past levels of profitability. The market for AI-focused CPUs is getting more competitive, with Arm Holdings and even Nvidia now coming in with new chips. Tim Arcuri of UBS sees Intel's gross margins reaching 50% by 2030 only under a "blue sky scenario." The company averaged an annual gross margin of over 60% from 2010 to 2020, according to data from S&P Global Market Intelligence.

In the near-term, Intel's data center business may also remain hampered by supply constraints cited in the last earnings call in January. That could pressure results for the first quarter that Intel will report on Thursday. That doesn't change the strong prospects for CPU chips over the longer term, but Intel isn't priced for even a moment of pain these days.

Write to Dan Gallagher at dan.gallagher@wsj.com

data.title: Inside the 'Financial Infidelities' That Tear Marriages Apart — WSJ
data.description:
By Gunjan Banerji | Photography by Michelle Gustafson for WSJ | Design by Annie Ng

This week, we're bringing you stories about the financial impact of divorce.

Marriages fall apart for any number of reasons. For Jessica Simon, the first signs that hers was in trouble showed up in the checking account she shared with her husband.

At times, it would be a stray DraftKings charge. On other days, there would be more. Roughly nine years into their marriage, her frustrations with the sports bets reached a tipping point.

The rift over money made clear how much they had both changed since they first started dating and had kids.

"We're not the same people we were," said Simon, 41, who lives in Philadelphia and works for a clinical research company.

Money, that eternal source of marital friction, offers a paper trail for the unraveling of countless marriages.

Roughly a third of Americans who have tied the knot said their first marriage ended in divorce, according to a Pew Research Center report that uses 2023 data. Divorce lawyers say that what's known as financial secrecy or infidelity is at the center of many splits. That can mean hiding when an income source dries up, keeping a secret bank account or giving in to any of the financial temptations that have proliferated in recent years, whether it's sports betting, shopping apps or risky trades.

"Many people are leading separate financial lives, or one spouse is in the dark," said Evan Schein, a partner at Berkman Bottger Newman & Schein in New York. "All of a sudden you might find out that the assets you thought existed, the marital pot that you thought was of one value, might be substantially less."

While many married couples still have joint accounts, more are opting to keep their finances separate, making it easier to conceal spending. The share of couples without any joint bank accounts rose to 23% in 2023, up from 15% in 1996, according to a Census survey last year.

The shift partly reflects the rising age of marriage for American men and women; individual bank accounts were more common for couples who married later.

In some cases, funds aren't just separate, but entirely hidden. When that happens, divorce lawyers can argue that one party was improperly spending assets that belonged to both spouses.

Marilyn Chinitz, a New York-based matrimonial attorney at Blank Rome, said one of her clients discovered a secret account kept by her ex-husband. A forensic accountant helped determine that he had been spending roughly $20,000 a month on another woman and their child in an apartment for around a decade. The accountant sifted through a mountain of documents to track transfers in and out of the account, uncovering a web of transactions that hadn't been disclosed.

"It's like taking a piece of fabric and pulling it and all of a sudden everything starts to unfold," Chinitz said.

Eventually, they tallied up millions spent on this hidden life.

Money secrets can lurk even when accounts are shared.

Gail Legaspi-Gaull said she and her husband pooled their money once they married. The 54-year-old, who lives in Virginia, said she had a higher salary and felt like she footed more of their expenses, including for their home and two kids.

Over time, she came to resent that. Then she noticed the balance on a bank account dwindling. It turned out his job situation had changed.

"He didn't even tell me at first," she said.

Jeff Gaull, her ex-husband, said he lost his full-time status but was still working. He says though his income decreased, he was still contributing to the household.

"I felt like if I told her right off the bat it would cause unnecessary stress on both of us," Gaull said.

He said her career often seemed like the priority in the relationship, something he felt they oriented their lives around. The money rift was one of several issues in their marriage, they both said, and over time they grew apart.

In some cases, letting your spouse take care of the finances can backfire. Tamsen Fadal, a podcast host and author, said her ex-husband handled a lot of the money. In retrospect, she wishes she had taken more control when they were together.

She occasionally worried if they could afford their lifestyle, but brushed aside her concerns.

When they went out to eat, for instance, she recalls ordering appetizers, main courses and desserts, racking up expensive bills.

"He's going to take care of it all," Fadal recalled thinking.

She eventually found out about an affair, she said, and they split. Soon after, she says she started opening bank statements that were arriving in the mail and was surprised to see an account she wasn't aware of. She says she was also in debt after she divorced.

"It really did change how I look at money," Fadal said.

She says she no longer outsources money decisions to her partner.

For Simon in Philadelphia, a flurry of sports bets she discovered one morning in 2023 set the divorce in motion, she said. To her, it was money they weren't saving or spending on things like family vacations. She wanted to control her own finances.

Shortly after, she opened her own bank account.

To receive Personal Finance email alerts, including stories in this series as soon as they publish, click here . Read the full series here .

Write to Gunjan Banerji at gunjan.banerji@wsj.com and Annie Ng at annie.ng@wsj.com
